items.subtopic: Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE
items.title: Why Early Diagnosis and Lifelong Treatment are Crucial in Wilson's Disease
items.content: Wilson's disease is an autosomal recessive disorder affecting copper transport, primarily impacting the liver and brain. Prompt diagnosis during presymptomatic or early symptomatic phases and lifelong treatment are essential to prevent premature mortality.
keyPoints: Autosomal recessive inherited disorder of copper transport
Primarily impacts the liver and brain
Prompt diagnosis in presymptomatic or early symptomatic phase is crucial
Lifelong treatment needed to prevent premature mortality
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE
items.title: Historical Milestones and Treatment Evolution of Wilson's Disease
items.content: The history of Wilson's disease includes its first description in 1912 by S.A.K. Wilson, the proposal of copper overload as a cause in 1948, and the introduction of various treatments over time, including D-penicillamine, triethylene tetramine, zinc salts, and liver transplants.
keyPoints: First described in 1912 by neurologist S.A.K. Wilson
Copper overload proposed as cause in 1948
D-penicillamine introduced in 1956 for copper chelation
Triethylene tetramine became second FDA-approved treatment in early 1970s
First liver transplants performed in early 1970s
Zinc salts recognized in early 1960s to reduce gastrointestinal copper absorption
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE

items.title: Age-related Presentation Patterns in Wilson's Disease
items.content: Wilson's disease presents differently based on age. Nearly all individuals with liver presentations are under 30 years old, while those with neurologic or psychiatric signs can range from the first to fifth decade.
keyPoints: Liver disease presentation typically occurs in individuals <30 years of age.
Neurologic and psychiatric presentations may occur from the first to the fifth decade.
Some degree of liver disease is invariably present regardless of clinical presentation.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.title: Diagnostic Importance of Kayser-Fleischer Rings in Wilson's Disease
items.content: The presence of Kayser-Fleischer rings, a golden to greenish-brown band in the peripheral cornea, is a pathognomonic sign for Wilson's disease. These rings are detected using slit-lamp or optical coherent tomography.
keyPoints: Kayser-Fleischer rings appear as a superior crescent and eventually become circumferential.
Approximately 95% of patients with neurologic signs have Kayser-Fleischer rings.
Two-thirds of those with hepatic presentations exhibit these rings.
Copper chelation therapy leads to fading and disappearance of the rings.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.title: Diagnostic Tests for Wilson's Disease: Biochemical Markers and Imaging
items.content: Wilson's disease diagnosis involves specific biochemical tests, including serum copper levels, ceruloplasmin incorporation of radiolabeled $^{64}	ext{Cu}$, urinary copper excretion, and liver biopsy. Each test provides critical insights into the presence of Wilson's disease.
keyPoints: Low serum copper and ceruloplasmin levels are indicative.
Elevated hepatic transaminase levels, aminoaciduria, and hemolytic anemia support diagnosis.
$^{64}	ext{Cu}$ incorporation test is highly specific; patients with Wilson's disease incorporate very little $^{64}	ext{Cu}$ into ceruloplasmin.
Urinary copper excretion >100 µg/24 h confirms the diagnosis, using acid-washed collection containers.
Penicillamine challenge shows several-fold increase in copper excretion during second 24-h urine collection after administration of 500 mg penicillamine.

Liver biopsy with hepatic copper values >200 µg/g dry weight (normal 20–50 µg) is a gold standard; preferred methods include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic absorption spectrometry.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Wilson's Disease: Molecular Insights and Diagnostic Scoring
items.content: The diagnosis of Wilson's disease also involves understanding the genetic basis and utilizing a scoring system to integrate clinical, biochemical, and molecular data.
keyPoints: Caused by loss-of-function variants in ATP7B; common missense variants include H1069Q, M645R, R778L.
ATP7B has >650 pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants with geographic, racial, and ethnic allelic frequency variations.
Prevalence estimates range from 1 in 7000 to 1 in 30,000, possibly due to incomplete penetrance and underdiagnosis.
Leipzig scoring system (2001) by EASL integrates clinical, biochemical, and molecular features for diagnosis.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Who first described Wilson's disease in 1912?
items.explanation: Wilson's disease was first described by the neurologist S.A.K. Wilson in 1912.

items.question: In what year was the gene for Wilson's disease identified?
items.explanation: The gene for Wilson's disease, ATP7B, was identified in 1993.

items.subtopic: Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE
items.question: Which treatment became the second FDA-approved therapy for Wilson's disease in the early 1970s?
items.explanation: Triethylene tetramine became the second FDA-approved treatment for Wilson's disease in the early 1970s.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE

items.question: Which age group most commonly presents with liver disease in Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Nearly all individuals who present with liver disease are <30 years of age.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.question: What is the pathognomonic sign of copper deposition in Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: The Kayser-Fleischer ring is a pathognomonic sign of copper deposition in Wilson's disease.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.question: Which neurologic feature is NOT typically associated with the presentation of Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Wilson's disease may be mistakenly diagnosed as Parkinson disease, but it is not typically associated with Parkinsonism.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.question: Which diagnostic test for Wilson's disease involves measuring the incorporation of radiolabeled copper into serum ceruloplasmin?
items.explanation: Incorporation of radiolabeled $^{64}\text{Cu}$ into serum ceruloplasmin is a specific diagnostic test for Wilson's disease.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the characteristic hepatic copper value in grams of dry weight that suggests Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Hepatic copper values >200 µg per gram of dry weight are characteristic of Wilson's disease.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which gene is associated with loss-of-function variants causing Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Wilson's disease is caused by loss-of-function variants in ATP7B.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The pathologist J.N. Cumings proposed an etiologic connection with copper overload for Wilson's disease in 1948.
items.explanation: J.N. Cumings did propose the connection between Wilson's disease and copper overload in 1948.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE
items.statement: The first liver transplants for Wilson's disease were performed in the late 1980s.
items.explanation: The first liver transplants for Wilson's disease were performed in the early 1970s.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · Wilson's Disease | HISTORY OF WILSON'S DISEASE

items.statement: Copper chelation therapy can lead to the disappearance of corneal copper deposits in Wilson's disease.
items.explanation: Copper chelation therapy causes fading and eventual disappearance of corneal copper.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.statement: Renal tubular dysfunction in Wilson's disease is unrelated to renal copper toxicity.
items.explanation: Renal tubular dysfunction is presumably related to renal copper toxicity.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · CLINICAL

items.statement: Large deletions are common in the ATP7B gene responsible for Wilson's disease.
items.explanation: Large deletions are much less common in ATP7B than in ATP7A.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The penicillamine challenge involves measuring copper excretion before and after administering penicillamine.
items.explanation: Penicillamine challenge uses serial urine copper measurements to diagnose Wilson's disease.
items.reference: Wilson s Disease · BIOCHEMICAL | MOLECULAR | DIAGNOSIS
